queue.label: Ad-hoc — RustDesk self-hosting cautionary tale
queue.notes: Ad-hoc draft. Ken to review and approve before posting. Written in Ken's first-person voice. Revised v2: removed Mac Mini M4 mention, added technical detail (hbbs/hbbr, port 21114 issue, RustDesk2.toml, Tailscale NAT), enriched learnings (know fallbacks upfront, know when to pivot, multiple paths exist). Revised v3: added irony layer — RustDesk's own docs recommend Docker as preferred; Ken followed them, which led him into the trap. Reinforces the lesson: don't trust official docs blindly when environment matters.
queue.draft:
I spent 3 hours today trying to save $20/month.

Self-hosted RustDesk. Docker. How hard could it be?

RustDesk's own docs recommend Docker as the preferred installation method. I followed them. First mistake.

RustDesk's self-hosted stack runs two components: hbbs (the rendezvous broker) and hbbr (the relay server). Straightforward on paper. On macOS with Docker Desktop, a different story.

The walls hit fast. macOS Application Firewall silently drops Docker UDP traffic — no error, no log, just nothing. Docker Desktop on macOS has known UDP port forwarding issues after any network change. The config file (RustDesk2.toml) gets overwritten on every app launch — you have to edit through the UI, not the file, and nobody tells you this. Then the 1.4.x client tries port 21114 (API server) before falling back to 21116 — but the open-source server doesn't include the API server. Firewall silently drops 21114. Client never falls back. Zero registrations. No error.

I spun up Tailscale. NAT traversal, no open ports, free tier. Ten minutes later, remote access was working.

Two things I'll carry forward.

Know your options before you start. Tailscale, Cloudflare Tunnel, WireGuard, a cloud-hosted RustDesk on Linux — these were all valid paths. Knowing your fallbacks upfront changes how you evaluate the primary option.

Know when to pivot. Chasing a fix only makes sense when you understand the effort-to-return ratio. A fallback in hand tells you exactly when the current path stops being worth it.

There's never just one way. Research the landscape first, then commit.

What's the most expensive "free" solution you've chased down a rabbit hole?

#SelfHosting #DevOps #RemoteAccess #CTO #LessonsLearned
